Well, welcome. Welcome to Lido Key. This is where I live. Actually, I live on the next island over. The Sarasota area, we're just in love with it and we're very proud of it. So it means a lot to us to host you guys here. We have that every night in our backyard. So you talk about a writer's life. I don't know. Maybe your fantasy is a mountain cabin. Maybe it's to live here. We could use more copywriters in Sarasota. But this is copywriting that made this lifestyle possible for us. A typical day for us. I'll get up at four in the morning. I'll work until about noon. Wendy and I will jump on our bicycles. We'll ride into Siesta Village. We'll grab a salad. I usually get a salad. And then ride back, ride around for a while. Just enjoy the day. Maybe go to the beach. Turn around, come home. Hang out in the pool until it's time for dinner and then we go back into town and do it again. And it's cool because Siesta is a little tiny village and the valets and the restaurant owners and everybody that know us really well and they treat us like kings. I mean, I actually had, we were having dinner at this one restaurant and the owner came over and handed me a full bottle of Gregus vodka and says, I want you to take this home with you. Just a gift. So it's perfect for us. It's the lifestyle we've always wanted. It's cool to live where people vacation or where they dream of vacationing. And so we're still in our season here. The spring breakers are still here. So the traffic's a little heavier than usual. But you can still get around pretty easily. We're in a, it is a little bit expensive to live here, which is another reason why the writer's life comes in really handy. Because the money's good. You know, once you get going, if you do it right, money can be awesome. And we're just in a rental because we're trying to sell our houses up north. But the house to the right of me is for sale right now for 11 million and the one to the left is for sale. There's an empty lot to the left of me that's up for sale for 4.4 million. So yeah, I know. But you know, it's worth it. You only live once, right? So now, I don't know, how many of you were at boot camp? All right. Well, you remember I asked a question, how many are you doing this for the money? And almost nobody raised their hands. And then I said, how many of you are doing it for the freedom? And everybody raised their hands, you know? And I said something like, well, it looks like we have a lot of liars in the room. But money and freedom really are the same thing. Without money, there is no freedom. Without money, you have no choice in life. Without money, you work for someone else. And they make all the determinations for you. Once you get to the point where you can accept the fact that money is good, I sound like Gordon Gekko here, you know, greed is good. But once you can get past that money is the root of all evil idea, I really think, you know, the Bible is a really good book and everything, but I think somebody inserted the word evil, okay? Money is really the root of all. Okay? You think about it. You think about it. Money is what you need in order to live today, to pay your bills, to provide shelter and food, education for your family. Okay? It's what you need tomorrow to have security for yourself so you don't have to work your entire life if you don't want to. I pretty much want to. I get bored over a long weekend, but if some of you get, well, like Carleen, she'll take a month off a couple of times a year and just go traveling, you know, with her kids. And she has the security, she can do that. It lets you buy toys. I'm kind of famous for my toys. I got a lot of them. I've got a Shelby GT500 that is running 750 horsepower. I've got an Indian motorcycle, Wendy rides a brand new Harley. We just sold a boat. We're going to probably buy another one as soon as we get rid of the other houses up north and buy here. So, you know, I love toys. I had a gun collection of 150 guns before we started downsizing it. So, you know, it's just, I'm not bragging. It's just stupid stuff you do when you have the money, when money is no object. But it brings a certain richness to life to be able to do that kind of stuff. Money is essential to relationships. More families break up over financial problems. And just about any other reason, any other excuse. Money gives you the ability to help people. You know, if anyone comes to you and acts all noble like, wow, you know, I don't care about money. You know, I care about all these other things. What he's saying is, I really don't care about being able to help people, including my own family or others in need in the community. Money gives you the ability to contribute to any cause that is important to you, whether it's a health cause, a political cause, or a religious cause. If those causes are important to you, then, you know, being a producer rather than a, what do you say, somebody that is just coasting along is essential. Now, the tip is, the first tip really for today is, you're really not going to be a top earner until you can be honest with yourself about the importance of money in your life. And to get, now see, I was raised by a Methodist minister. So that whole idea of money as a root of all evil was deeply ingrained in me, and I had to get rid of that pretty quick. So here's how we're going to do this today. I've counted six things that I think have contributed to my success in terms of my financial success, in terms of being able to make a lot of money. Now, to me, how much is a lot of money? Well, I had a year, a couple of years ago, where I worked real hard and brought in $4 million. You know, I had a month where I brought in $1 million in one month. Wendy and I, actually it was 1.8, and Wendy and I bought a lake house and a bunch of furniture with that money. Carline's been there. Yeah. Up in Atlanta. So the first thing is to perfect your craft. Now, I know all of you guys have studied copywriting, and you're continuing, many of you are continuing to study it. And that's great. I'm still studying it. I read the Masters every year. I talk to other copywriters to figure out what's working. You're a perpetual student once you start down this road, because the market changes. Now, human beings don't change, but the market will change. We'll talk about some of those changes today. So we're going to spend two sessions on this, correcting your craft. You have to be able to write good, solid copy faster so you can do more projects per year. Right? Makes sense, common sense. More projects, more money. You have to attract great clients. You've got to get people who pay you and who have products that give you the opportunity to really shine and generate big revenue increases. You need to have the marketers' mindset. There's a lot that goes into just the psychology of being a copywriter that we're going to cover this weekend. You need to make sure that you're paid fairly, that you know how to structure win-win deals. And you need to know how to make the most amount of money possible with each client you work with. So this is not necessarily about working harder or working longer hours. I'm sure I don't work longer hours than any of the other copywriters in this room or any other copywriter that I've met. It's about working smarter. I'll give you an example. I was talking to Gary Benzavanga who to all of us is kind of a God, right? And I told him about something I had done with a client in terms of one of our deals. And he just about, you know, fell out of his chair. He said, I just wish I was that smart. Okay? It was just a simple idea. Now Gary is a guy who one time was given a royalty check of three and a half million dollars by Rodale. They made him fly into town, sit in their office and handed him the check over the desk. And the guy who handed in the check was in my office a couple of years later and said it was the hardest thing he ever did. But he knew, you know, that, you know, he had to do it because he'd lose Gary if he didn't. So again, these are the sessions that we're going to do. The first one is on perfecting your craft. The second one is how to write hot copy faster. I love picking up. Picking up the pictures is my favorite part of this whole. How to get great clients to beat a path to your door. Now the thing here is there are two parts of this part is great clients. We're going to talk about what makes a great client, but also how to get them to come to you instead of always having to beat the bushes for the next client. The marketers mindset, we talked about that. Come on, baby. Okay. This is the second part of the perfecting your craft part. Structuring win-win deals. And there are two parts of that too. Part one is making sure that you get a deal, your first deal with a new client that really is a win-win for both of you. So he comes away from it very, very happy. And so do you. But the longer term view is very important. We're going to get into that in detail, which has to do with turning that client into a lifetime client. We're going to, oh, this is going to be, I should have scheduled this for early morning instead of after lunch, but we have to go through it because this is stuff, there's some stuff that these clients will put in contracts for some, you know, some of the time that you can just say no, you know. And there's other stuff that you need to insist is in there. And it has to do with protecting you against any regulatory problems, against any lawsuits that might be caused by the copy and so forth. And then finally, this one is going to be a lot of fun. It basically just has to do with, okay, you've got a client now, what? How do you turn this guy into someone who's going to pay you every month for the rest of your life? And we're going to hit the high points on that one. It's pretty, there's a lot of detail there. So that's why we included Wendy, is it the Make Peace Business System? That has to do with marketing nuts and bolts. Yeah. Make Peace Business System. It's important as a copywriter to understand what your client is looking at. When you write the copy, you send out a package or email or write a sales page or whatever, how is he evaluating whether it's successful or not? Or what were his goals in the first place in terms of what he wants to accomplish? And so that gets down into some number crunching and stuff, and we'll go through that so you're equipped to have that conversation with the client. So counting me, there are eight top copywriters in this room right now. Six of us, seven of us, well probably all of us are over six figures, okay? And there are several of us who are over seven a year, over a million dollars a year in income. They came here without talking about compensation, just to be here for you guys. So if you have any questions, if you have an idea, you want to bounce off of one of us, you can certainly do it with these people.
